next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The seventh day was the worst.

Not because the Mark hurt more, though it did. The magic in it kept reaching for the diagnostic touch that was no longer there. The seventh day was worst because the house had finally stopped trying.

For six days the Manor had managed something. Not the responsive architecture he had grown used to when she was here. No shortcuts, no corridors rearranging themselves, no rooms surfacing like suggestions. There had been small signs instead: a sconce flickering in the east wing, a door that stuck and then released, a contraction in the hallway outside the library like the building clearing its throat. Signs of life, if he was generous. Last autonomic functions, if he was honest.

On the seventh day, nothing. The Manor held its shape. A house that moved was a house with opinions. This was just a house.

At ten in the morning Draco stood at the library entrance, dressed because dressing still divided one hour from the next. Trousers, shirt, shoes, the costume of someone with somewhere to go. The door had been open all week. Beyond it the windows let in what December offered, which was barely anything: flat grey light and an unbroken sheet of cloud. He had lost track of whether it was the second or the third of the month.

The archive wall gave him blank stone. His palm went to it anyway, part habit and part test. Cold met skin. Not the pointed cold of a house choosing to withhold. Just winter in old stone.

Warming charms would have helped. He had not bothered.

What he missed was not only her touch on his arm, though his body kept a...
open_hooks_to_carry: Hermione in her flat: the ordinary silence, the absence of the Manor's responsiveness, the gutted feeling
Mark resonance persists at distance — she can feel his pain, the Mark thrashing without her sustained contact
Magical decay accelerating: Floo down for hours, accidental magic spikes, institutional crisis
Harry's honest line: the thing she is angry about is not what she thinks it is
She sits with the truth of what she concealed and why
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to grey, then into the early dark that came before anyone was ready for it. The corridor outside the library shortened by several feet, then stretched again, the house failing for a moment to settle around her absence.

Pain tore through his left forearm, the old pain this time, nearer to a burn than the recent low ache, dark magic looking for a command that would never come. It hit hard enough to make him fold over in the chair, one hand locked around his wrist.

So that was back.

He stayed there until the worst of the wave passed. When he lifted his head again the library had lost the coherence he'd mistaken for architecture. The tables and shelves were still in place, but the room no longer knew what to do with itself.

On the table, her notes were still spread around the ink stain. In the archive behind the hidden wall, the damaged folio still lay open to the ruined clause that had broken them.

Maybe the missing word would've cleared her. Maybe it would've condemned both of them. It changed nothing about the fact that she'd known there was doubt in the room and let him walk into it blind.

The dark pressed harder at the windows. Somewhere deeper in the house, a door shut by itself.

Before she came, cold had been ordinary here. That was the shape of his life and he'd learned it. After five and a half weeks of her footsteps in the corridor, her books on the chairs, her voice crossing the library at all hours, the old cold no longer fit. It felt wrong on his skin, borrowed, like the house was handing him back a life he had already, stupidly, stopped knowing how to live.
secondary_character_beats: Harry as the person who has earned the right to say hard things. His line should cut through her self-narrative without being cruel
state_deltas_required: Separation. The Mark is worse without her sustained contact. The magical decay accelerates. The research is unfinished. The cost of apart is concrete
The cost of separation is measurable — personal, magical, structural. Hermione begins to understand what she did and why. Harry says the hard true thing
